Sketch walkthrough

Polyuria — Water vs Solute Diuresis & DI Subtypes

🎯 Look at the lit symbol — say out loud what it is and what it represents (and the trap). Then reveal.

What you see
What it encodes
The board trap
Use ← → arrow keys · click a dot to jump · ▶ plays & narrates the scene
⚠️ Board traps 14
  1. Polyuria > 3 L/24h — Urinary frequency is NOT polyuria — small frequent voids point to bladder/prostate, not a renal concentrating defect; measure the 24h volume.
  2. Urine osmolality splits the diagnosis — A dilute urine (<300) excludes osmotic diuresis — don't chase glucose/mannitol when the urine is hypotonic; that's a water-handling problem.
  3. Water diuresis — Water diuresis means dilute urine — high urine osmolality rules it OUT and points instead to a solute (osmotic) load.
  4. Solute / osmotic diuresis — Osmotic diuresis from glucosuria/urea won't correct with DDAVP — fix the solute load, don't give vasopressin.
  5. Excessive nonabsorbed solute — glucose, mannitol, urea — High-protein tube feeds cause a urea osmotic diuresis with rising BUN — mistaken for DI if you don't check the solute load.
  6. Damaged/resisting tubule — Bartter, cystic, post-obstructive ATN — Post-obstructive and recovering-ATN diuresis is self-limited tubular wasting — over-replacing fluids perpetuates it; match output, don't chase it.
  7. Water deprivation chamber — Water deprivation is being supplanted by copeptin testing — and is dangerous in true DI (risk of hypernatremia); monitor weight and Na closely.
  8. Central DI room — AVP-sensitive — Central DI responds robustly to DDAVP; nephrogenic does not — the DDAVP response, not the deprivation alone, separates them.
  9. Nephrogenic DI room — AVP-insensitive — Failure to respond to DDAVP = nephrogenic, not central — giving more desmopressin won't help; remove the offending drug/electrolyte.
  10. Lithium — most common acquired nephrogenic DI — In lithium NDI, amiloride (not more thiazide alone) blocks ENaC-mediated lithium uptake — and never restrict water in a polyuric DI patient.
  11. Primary polydipsia — Primary polydipsia gives LOW serum Na, whereas DI tends toward HIGH-normal/elevated Na — the baseline sodium distinguishes them before any test.
  12. Hypercalcemia / hypokalemia → nephrogenic — Polyuria from hypercalcemia mimics primary DI but reverses once calcium normalizes — check Ca/K before labeling it idiopathic.
  13. 24h urine collection confirms — Skipping the 24h volume risks working up 'DI' in a patient who merely has frequency — confirm the volume first.
  14. Alarm panel — diabetic/volume depletion — Glucosuric osmotic diuresis won't respond to DDAVP and shows urine osm >300 — treat the hyperglycemia, not a presumed water-handling defect.
📖 Read-through — all 14 symbols
1. Polyuria > 3 L/24h
Polyuria is urine output >3 L/day, distinct from frequency (small volumes) and nocturia. Confirm with a 24-hour collection before workup. Splits into water diuresis vs solute (osmotic) diuresis by urine osmolality.
⚠️ Urinary frequency is NOT polyuria — small frequent voids point to bladder/prostate, not a renal concentrating defect; measure the 24h volume.
2. Urine osmolality splits the diagnosis
Urine osm <300 mOsm/kg = water diuresis (DI or primary polydipsia); >300 with high solute excretion = solute diuresis. This single number is the first fork in the polyuria algorithm.
⚠️ A dilute urine (<300) excludes osmotic diuresis — don't chase glucose/mannitol when the urine is hypotonic; that's a water-handling problem.
3. Water diuresis
Water diuresis = large volumes of dilute (hypotonic) urine from DI (can't concentrate) or primary polydipsia (suppressed AVP). >24h urine collection confirms; water-deprivation/copeptin testing separates the causes.
⚠️ Water diuresis means dilute urine — high urine osmolality rules it OUT and points instead to a solute (osmotic) load.
4. Solute / osmotic diuresis
Solute diuresis = >600–900 mOsm/day excreted, dragging water out: hyperglycemia (glucose), mannitol, high-protein tube feeds (urea), post-obstructive, radiocontrast. Urine osm stays >300.
⚠️ Osmotic diuresis from glucosuria/urea won't correct with DDAVP — fix the solute load, don't give vasopressin.
5. Excessive nonabsorbed solute — glucose, mannitol, urea
Classic osmotic loads: uncontrolled diabetes (glucose), mannitol infusion, high-protein enteral feeding and radiocontrast (urea). Each delivers nonreabsorbed solute to the tubule, obligating water loss.
⚠️ High-protein tube feeds cause a urea osmotic diuresis with rising BUN — mistaken for DI if you don't check the solute load.
6. Damaged/resisting tubule — Bartter, cystic, post-obstructive ATN
Tubular injury impairs reabsorption: Bartter/Gitelman, medullary cystic disease, recovering ATN, and post-obstructive diuresis all cause inappropriate salt/water wasting. Large urine output may exceed 3–6 L/day.
⚠️ Post-obstructive and recovering-ATN diuresis is self-limited tubular wasting — over-replacing fluids perpetuates it; match output, don't chase it.
7. Water deprivation chamber
Water deprivation withholds fluid to see if endogenous AVP concentrates the urine; failure to concentrate then a response to DDAVP indicates central DI, while no response indicates nephrogenic DI.
⚠️ Water deprivation is being supplanted by copeptin testing — and is dangerous in true DI (risk of hypernatremia); monitor weight and Na closely.
8. Central DI room — AVP-sensitive
Central DI = deficient AVP from hypothalamic/posterior pituitary damage (trauma, surgery, tumor, infiltration, infection). Urine osm rises >50% after DDAVP. Treat with desmopressin.
⚠️ Central DI responds robustly to DDAVP; nephrogenic does not — the DDAVP response, not the deprivation alone, separates them.
9. Nephrogenic DI room — AVP-insensitive
Nephrogenic DI = kidney resistance to AVP. Acquired causes (lithium, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, pyelonephritis) and rare congenital V2-receptor/AQP2 defects. Minimal urine concentration after DDAVP.
⚠️ Failure to respond to DDAVP = nephrogenic, not central — giving more desmopressin won't help; remove the offending drug/electrolyte.
10. Lithium — most common acquired nephrogenic DI
Chronic lithium is the most common acquired cause of nephrogenic DI; it enters principal cells via ENaC and downregulates AQP2. Amiloride blocks lithium entry; stop lithium if possible.
⚠️ In lithium NDI, amiloride (not more thiazide alone) blocks ENaC-mediated lithium uptake — and never restrict water in a polyuric DI patient.
11. Primary polydipsia
Primary (psychogenic/dipsogenic) polydipsia = excess water intake suppresses AVP, causing dilute polyuria with LOW-normal serum sodium. Urine concentrates appropriately with deprivation; AVP/copeptin are appropriately low.
⚠️ Primary polydipsia gives LOW serum Na, whereas DI tends toward HIGH-normal/elevated Na — the baseline sodium distinguishes them before any test.
12. Hypercalcemia / hypokalemia → nephrogenic
Hypercalcemia and hypokalemia impair medullary concentrating ability and AQP2 trafficking, producing reversible acquired nephrogenic DI. Correct the electrolyte to restore concentrating capacity.
⚠️ Polyuria from hypercalcemia mimics primary DI but reverses once calcium normalizes — check Ca/K before labeling it idiopathic.
13. 24h urine collection confirms
Always document true polyuria (>3 L/day) with a timed collection before extensive testing. Separates genuine polyuria from frequency/nocturia and anchors the water-vs-solute fork.
⚠️ Skipping the 24h volume risks working up 'DI' in a patient who merely has frequency — confirm the volume first.
14. Alarm panel — diabetic/volume depletion
Uncontrolled diabetes is the prototypical solute diuresis: glucosuria obligates water and electrolyte loss, causing volume depletion and possibly large urine output. Glycemic control resolves it.
⚠️ Glucosuric osmotic diuresis won't respond to DDAVP and shows urine osm >300 — treat the hyperglycemia, not a presumed water-handling defect.
